prediction.title: Population pharmacokinetic analysis for 10-monohydroxy derivative of oxcarbazepine in pediatric epileptic patients shows no difference between Japanese and other ethnicities

structural_model.description: One-compartment model with first-order absorption and elimination

omega.description: Between-subject variability in CL/F and V/F covariance

sigma.description: Residual error model for MHD concentrations

covariates: concomitant AEDs (carbamazepine, phenobarbital, phenytoin): qCBZ,CL/F, qPB,CL/F, qPHT,CL/F
evidence_quotes: No clinically relevant differences were found for using body surface area or weight to explain between-patient variability, therefore the final model included the effects of body weight on apparent clearance (CL/F) and apparent volume of distribution (V/F) of MHD, and in addition, the effect of 3 concomitant anti-epileptic drugs (carbamazepine, phenobarbital and phenytoin) on CL/F of MHD.

In Step 2 and 3, the pooled data from the studies in Japanese and non-Japanese was used for the analysis. The final model included the effects of body weight on CL/F, 3 concomitant AEDs (carbamazepine, phenobarbital and phenytoin) on CL/F and body weight on V/F.

No significant change in OFV was observed after adding ethnicity on CL/F, which resulted in OFV changing from 3026.477 to 3026.362 (DOFV: -0.115, p = 0.735). The estimated ratio in CL/F between ethnicities was 1.01 (95% confidence interval: 0.931e1.09). Therefore, we concluded that there was no ethnic difference in CL/F of MHD between Japanese and non-Japanese pediatric patients.
prediction.notes: The model is based on a one-compartment structure with first-order absorption and elimination. The final model includes the effects of body weight on clearance (CL/F) and volume of distribution (V/F), as well as the effect of concomitant AEDs on CL/F. Ethnicity was not found to significantly affect PK parameters.
